name: cross_engine_portability_pg_to_mysql
description: Cross-engine portability analysis: a transaction workload that is safe under PostgreSQL Serializable (SSI) but may exhibit anomalies under MySQL REPEATABLE READ due to different concurrency control mechanisms. IsoSpec detects this portability violation.
references: Adya. Weak Consistency: A Generalized Theory and Optimistic Implementations. PhD thesis, MIT, 1999
Kleppmann. Hermitage: Testing the 'I' in ACID. 2014
setup_sql: CREATE TABLE inventory (item_id INT PRIMARY KEY, warehouse VARCHAR(32) NOT NULL, quantity INT NOT NULL)

workload.description: Transfer between warehouses with availability check. T1 transfers from east to west, T2 transfers from west to east. Both check total availability first.

postgresql_serializable.outcome: SAFE — SSI detects rw-dependency cycle and aborts one transaction
postgresql_serializable.mechanism: SIREAD lock tracking detects T1 rw→ T2 rw→ T1 dangerous structure
postgresql_serializable.final_state: Consistent: one transfer completes, other retries

mysql_repeatable_read.outcome: UNSAFE — write skew possible under snapshot isolation without SSI
mysql_repeatable_read.mechanism: MySQL RR uses snapshot reads; no rw-dependency tracking
mysql_repeatable_read.final_state: Both transfers succeed on stale snapshots. East: 10-5+8=13, West: 10+5-8=7 OR East: 10+8-5=13, West: 10-8+5=7 — depending on commit order, but both saw total=20 when only 20 exists
mysql_repeatable_read.note: The write-skew anomaly is not prevented because MySQL RR does not implement SSI

isospec_verdict.recommendation: Use MySQL SERIALIZABLE (full 2PL) or add SELECT ... FOR UPDATE to enforce locking reads
